justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO-monitoring gap — called out as 'CRITICAL' and 'single most important missing metric'. (2) Retry tracking gap identified with detail on missing retry_attempts_total, queue depth, and oldest message age. (3) Current alert explicitly critiqued as symptom-based — noted it fires on API errors but cannot answer whether SLOs are being met. (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, retry_attempts_total counter, retry_queue_depth gauge, retry_queue_oldest_message_age_seconds gauge, renewals_scheduled_total counter. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — references multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts per Google SRE practices. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with failure mode analysis, reconciliation checks, circuit breaker recommendations, and on-call readiness assessment
Correctly frames the core problem: 'two explicit SLOs but ZERO metrics that directly measure either SLO'
Distinguishes between log-based forensics and real-time metric alerting — notes logs are not a substitute for metrics without a log-to-metric pipeline
Provides concrete alert thresholds tied to SLO budgets (p95 lag > 30min = 50% of 1-hour budget, oldest retry age > 12 hours = 50% of 24-hour budget)
Identifies the shallow health check as a false-confidence risk — a stalled scheduler or dead retry consumer would not be detected
3am incident scenario effectively illustrates why the current monitoring is insufficient for operational response

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO-monitoring gap — explicitly calls out renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram as the #1 priority. (2) Retry tracking gap identified — calls retry mechanism 'a complete black box' and recommends retry_attempts_total, retry_exhausted_total. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — notes the 10-errors-in-5-minutes threshold is 'too simplistic', could fire on normal noise or miss real SLO breaches, and explicitly distinguishes it from SLO-based alerting. (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, renewal_slo_violations_total, renewal_retry_attempts_total, renewal_retry_exhausted_total, queue_depth gauge, and more. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — explicitly recommends 'multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting' and burn-rate alerts. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough identification of the scheduling lag gap as the core unmeasured SLO, with clear explanation of why it matters
Strong critique of the existing alert as an anti-pattern — absolute error count vs error rate, and symptom vs SLO distinction clearly drawn
Retry tracking gap comprehensively covered with specific metric recommendations and business impact (lost revenue)
Goes beyond minimum requirements with valuable additions: queue depth monitoring, stale processor detection, circuit breaker recommendations, and on-call documentation gaps
Excellent debugging capability framing — describes what an on-call engineer CAN vs CANNOT answer, making the gaps concrete and actionable
Confidence section is well-calibrated, clearly separating high-confidence findings from assumptions about unspecified implementation details

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO-monitoring gap — explicitly called out as 'MISSING: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds' and labeled 'the ONLY way to measure the 1-hour SLO'. (2) Retry tracking gap identified — retry_attempts_total, retry_lag_seconds, and retry_queue_depth all called out as missing, with clear link to the 24-hour retry SLO being unmeasurable. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — escalation explicitly states the error-rate alert 'does not detect the most dangerous failure mode: silent processing delays where no errors are thrown.' (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, retry_attempts_total counter with labels, retry_lag_seconds histogram, renewal_slo_violations_total counter, retry_queue_depth gauge. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — calls for SLO compliance dashboard and SLO-based alert rules. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough identification of the scheduling lag gap — not just naming the missing metric but explaining why it's the only way to measure the 1-hour SLO
Strong critique of the existing alert as symptom-based: clearly articulates that it may not fire during silent delays and may fire when renewals are still succeeding
Retry tracking gap covered comprehensively — identifies missing retry_attempts_total, retry_lag_seconds, and retry_queue_depth as a complete blind spot
Goes beyond minimum requirements with excellent failure mode analysis: stalled scheduler, dead retry consumer, poison messages, clock skew
Contract compliance section powerfully frames the gap: 'two explicit SLOs but ZERO metrics that measure compliance with either SLO'
Practical on-call perspective — identifies the three questions an engineer can't answer during an incident, grounding the gap analysis in operational reality
